author: @blackseabrew
text: One idea that is a bit more high tech would be to fabricate a current collector via vapor deposition. I'm thinking of my experience with flexible printed circuits for printer cartridges. My reasoning is that vapor deposition requires very little metal and it's not terribly difficult to accomplish although it's not a home laboratory piece of kit. I'm wondering if a taking the thin film circuit out of a print cartridge could be cut in an appropriate way to test this idea.

author: @VenkateshksDr
text:
Graphite is almost like metal with sheet structure and a sea of electrons floating about.  Though not freely as in metals and the structure is not strictly lattice but is sort of pseudo lattice due to the ever present interfering Van Der Waals forces.:)
The other electrode is carbon with an irregular structure.  This consideration along with that of the P-N junction idea may point out to the right direction.

author: @hirnlager
text: I opened a Bettery and i see in it black "Powder", its that Corbon, can i use it to make Graphene?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it depends on the battery - sometimes it's carbon, sometimes manganese dioxide, sometimes it's graphite and sometimes a mix of the three and it is always mixed with other chemicals - it's not a good materials unless you separate it out and wash it - you can use carbon to make graphene but it is difficult - much easier to make it from graphite only

author: @sasjadv
text:
Nice!
What will happen if you put this under (high) pressure?
I think it should either give a higher voltage or short out and the volts will drop.
If air acts as a separator and the air goes away because of the pressure it should stop generating a voltage, if that's not the case you have something even more interesting.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i am painting up a multilayer device right now

replies.author: @sasjadv
replies.text:
I have to admit that talking about air sound strange but if there is a monolithic layer of something water proof, how can the electrolyte reach both plates? And possible air trapped between isn't purely hydrophilic nor hydrophobic, water can to some degree dissolve in air.
That's why I thought about air bubbles, otherwise I have no clue about what's going on.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text:
this seriously needs to be tested as a sealed system with an array of them :) I wish you were closer so I could send some of the materials I have collected here, that are plastics you can wick things into like paper but will not break down like paper might,

The Post Office here makes their shipping envelopes for express mailing out of Tyvek which is Polyethylene if I recall and is a fiber so it lets liquid pass through it, so that might work for your project here, and allow you to really stack the crap out of a batch of them then seal them up once soaked to see how long that works :)


I definitely need to get some of the stuff you have been making like the conductive ink to do some experiments :) (eventually want to make some of my own, as I love learning how the stuff works first hand :) )


Thanks for sharing this for sure :) also thanks for joining us on the radio show today earlier :) wish you could join us on our google hangouts show tonight, maybe one day? lol

author: @joeglory425
text: Put it on a hot plate and check to see if output improves.  It will probably wrinkle as it dries out so place some sort of non conductive weight on top of it.  I imagine if it's battery behavior then output should rise as it warms and then drop once it's dried.  If it's because of P-N junctions responding to infrared then the output should continue to climb the warmer it gets.

author: @lukelordoflight
text:
Hello Robert!

There was a company,  called Power Japan Plus, that did just release a dual carbon battery, with active material being only carbon, but I guess they used a separator...

I was writing to Mr. John Bedini, the inventor of the Alum Battery, regarding a dual carbon battery, with only Alum as electrolyte, but did not got an answer back until now...
Perhaps you can test it..
Heat alum until it melts, adding only a few drops of H2O, and place it in between the carbon electrodes ...would be curious, if this will work better than backing soda stuff..

Looking forward to your conclusion :)

Thanks for your email... can't wait to see you make a full wall painted heater :)
Thanks and kind regards!

Marcel

author: @pixelspring
text: I've been toying around with p - n junctions... and ..... not sure of course.. but could be i reckon.  2 types of carbon in 2 different matrices... 2 'semi' -conductors.  Have you tried stacking them voltaic pile style , multi layer to bring up the voltage ?  ... Nice inventing :)   also though perhaps the paper itself is playing a larger role.... ... this link below is interesting ... i wonder... ... they say the nano structure of the paper fibres helps to create nano wires of carbon from the conductive ink as it seeps into the paper and then dries.... that is kind of cool when you think about it .... i am guessing perhaps the paper acts as a matrix that the conductive ink can seep down along by capillary action before it then dries solid into that form.  presto - carbon nano-wires of superconductive graphine...   Reckon you are really onto something here Robert.   /watch?v=QPTcQJPbGHw .  good stuff.  :)
